title: Pilgrim at Tinker Creek
excerpt: A visionary meditation on nature, terror, and transcendence - watching a Virginia creek with the eyes of a mystic and the precision of a naturalist.
keyIdeas: Nature is not only beautiful but violent, grotesque, and terrifying
The natural world is a theophany - a revelation of the divine in the ordinary
Seeing is a spiritual discipline that requires patience and humility
The apparent meaninglessness of nature's cruelty is itself a kind of mystery
keyTakeaways: A frog being drained dry by a giant water bug is as real a part of nature as a sunset
The praying mantis's mating cannibalism raises unanswerable questions about design and purpose
Human consciousness is both a gift and a burden - we alone can witness and wonder
Nature does not answer our moral questions; it simply is
whoShouldRead: Readers of Thoreau who want a darker, more contemporary Walden
Students of American nature writing and literary nonfiction
Anyone wrestling with questions of suffering, meaning, and the natural world
Mystics, skeptics, and everyone in between
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking a calm, reassuring nature book
Those disturbed by detailed descriptions of predation and parasitism
